title: Euler-Maruyama method for non-Wiener processes
abstract: Descriptions of complex physical or biological systems often include stochastic contributions, and these are commonly simulated using Wiener processes. In many cases however, non-Gaussian fluctuations may originate from non-Wiener processes which remain less explored. The Euler-Maruyama method of discretising stochastic differential equations to non-Wiener processes is generalised. Non-Gaussian noise generated from a subset of Lévy processes can be used simply and often with more physical justification, for both additive and multiplicative noise. An example of this is provided that gives superior physical results compared to using geometric Brownian motion. Finally the results of the additive noise are shown to be equivalent to a derived master equation via the Kramers-Moyal expansion.
summary: This paper introduces a generalized Euler-Maruyama method for simulating stochastic differential equations driven by non-Wiener, non-Gaussian processes. By using increments from Lévy processes like the Gamma distribution, the method more accurately captures the physical reality of systems with skewed fluctuations. It demonstrates that this approach avoids the inaccuracies introduced by standard Gaussian approximations in modeling decay and diffusion.
main_problem: The paper addresses the limitations of the standard Euler-Maruyama method when modeling complex systems that exhibit non-Gaussian (non-Wiener) fluctuations.
main_result: The author develops a 'relaxed' Euler-Maruyama method using Lévy processes (such as Gamma processes) that accurately captures the tails and skewness of non-Gaussian noise, providing more physically accurate results for decay processes than standard Brownian motion.
method: A generalized Euler-Maruyama discretization scheme where increments are drawn from non-Gaussian, infinitely divisible distributions, validated via Kramers-Moyal expansion and comparison with the Central Limit Theorem approach.
model_or_system: The method is applied to stochastic differential equations (SDEs) featuring both additive and multiplicative noise, specifically modeling noisy exponential decay and particle decay processes.
key_observables: Probability density functions (PDFs), mean lifetime, variance, skewness, and kurtosis.
important_parameters: Simulation time step (delta t), decay rates (nu and lambda), and noise variance (sigma^2).
main_assumptions: The method assumes the noise follows a subset of Lévy processes that are infinitely divisible; numerical implementation of the Master equation requires truncation of the Kramers-Moyal expansion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares PDFs of different processes across methods; Figure 2 shows decay trajectories and the distribution of mean lifetimes; Figure 3 compares the Master equation solution against the exact EM method solution.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the mathematical requirements for a relaxed EM method, moves to testing the method with additive noise models, and concludes with applications to multiplicative noise and validation via the Kramers-Moyal expansion.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics who need to simulate non-equilibrium processes where environmental noise is non-Gaussian or heavy-tailed.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Probability density functions of the reaction-diffusion system for different L´evy processes, comparing the naive (n), Euler-Maruyama (em), and central limit theorem (clt) meth- ods. The letter refers to Table. (I), whilst the number refers to the time step length.

FIG. 2. Modeling of noisy exponential decay. a) Examples of decays for the standard Wiener process (w) and gamma pro- cess (g). b) The measured mean lifetime of the simulations. The number refers to the time step.
FIG. 3. a) Solution of equation Eq. (6) compared to the exact solution for an initial delta distribution at x = 0. b) for additive noise, compared to using EM method.
